sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on the Australian pecan industry, Australian state agriculture departments, United States university pecan extension (the global reference for the crop), and the WANATCA and Rare Fruit Council of Australia archives.

core.intro: A pecan is a big, long-lived deciduous nut tree that asks for two things above all: a long, hot summer to ripen and fill the nuts, and a second, compatible variety nearby for pollination. Get those right, give it deep soil and plenty of summer water, and a single tree will reward you with nuts for generations. The state sections below cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in Australia.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted variety, not an unnamed seedling. Australian nurseries group pecans by pollination type, usually labelled Type A (also called Type I, or protandrous) and Type B (also called Type II, or protogynous), and the trick to a good crop is to plant one of each (see the next section). Among the varieties sold here, Cape Fear, Cherokee and Desirable are Type A, while Wichita, Mahan and Shoshoni are Type B, so a Cape Fear with a Wichita, for example, makes a sound pair. Pabst is sold as self-pollinating and is the usual pick when there is room for only one tree, though even it crops better with a partner. Riverside is a cheap seedling rather than a grafted variety, so it is slower to bear and its nuts are less predictable.

sections.heading: Pollination: plant a Type A and a Type B
sections.body: Pecans carry male catkins and female flowers on the same tree, but the two rarely shed and receive at the same time: a Type A (protandrous) variety sheds its pollen before its own female flowers are ready, and a Type B (protogynous) variety does the reverse. Planting one of each so their timing overlaps is what gives a heavy, well-filled crop. Pollen travels on the wind, not by bees, so the two trees need to be reasonably close together. A lone tree can self-pollinate after a fashion, but self-pollinated nuts often fill poorly or drop, so a single tree gives a light, unreliable crop. If you truly have room for only one, choose a variety sold as self-pollinating and accept a smaller yield.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Pecans need full sun and a lot of room. They are large trees, commonly 10 to 20 m tall and almost as wide in the open, very long-lived (a century or more), and they drive down a deep taproot, so plant them well clear of buildings, paths, sewer lines and overhead wires. They evolved on the deep, fertile, well-drained loams of river flats and crop best in soil at least 2 m deep; they will tolerate poorer, sandy soils but grow and bear less there. The pecan is an adaptable tree that grows across a wide range of climates and soils, but the common thread in every good pecan district is a hot summer. Container-grown trees go in through the cooler months while you can keep the water up (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Pecans are thirsty trees and their peak demand is during nut filling in late summer, when the kernels are swelling; water stress at that point ruins the kernels and the yield, so deep, regular watering through summer is essential away from reliable rain. At the same time they will not tolerate waterlogging, which rots the roots, which is why deep, free-draining soil matters even though the wild tree grows on river flats. Pecans are also famously hungry for zinc: the classic deficiency shows as small, crinkled, yellow-mottled new leaves (called rosette or little leaf) and is common on alkaline soils where zinc is locked up, so correct it with foliar zinc sprays in spring and keep the soil slightly acid to neutral. As a rule of thumb, a healthy mature tree puts on about 20 to 40 cm of new shoot growth a year; less than that means it wants more feeding and water.

sections.heading: Harvest, cracking and storing
sections.body: Pecans flower in spring and then need a long, hot summer (roughly 180 to 220 frost-free days) to ripen, so the nuts mature in autumn. As the green outer husk dries and splits it releases the familiar hard-shelled nut; some varieties drop their nuts, others hang on and are knocked down with poles or, in orchards, shaken from the tree. Once gathered, crack the shells and lift out the kernels, which freeze well in sealed bags for up to a year. A useful quirk for Australian growers is that this autumn harvest falls in the northern hemisphere's off season, which is why local nuts find a ready export market.

sections.heading: A note on pecan scab: good news for Australia
sections.body: Pecan scab, caused by the fungus Fusicladium effusum, is the single most damaging pecan disease in the humid growing regions of the world and forces heavy fungicide programs overseas. Australia is currently free of it, which is a real advantage: pecans here are grown largely without fungicides. Scab is an exotic, notifiable disease, which is one reason live pecan trees and nuts face quarantine when they cross some state borders. Because the fungus thrives in warm, wet, humid weather, were it ever to arrive it would press hardest on the humid coast and least on the dry inland, so dry-summer districts are the safest places to grow the crop.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree. Pecans do not come true from seed, so a cheap seedling gives unpredictable nuts and takes far longer to bear, while a grafted, named variety fruits sooner and to type. Check the label for the variety's pollination type (A or I, against B or II) so you can pair two compatible trees, and remember the tree's eventual size when you choose a spot. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How long until a pecan tree bears nuts?
faqs.a: A grafted, named pecan usually sets its first small crop in about five to eight years and builds to full production from roughly ten to fifteen years, after which it keeps cropping for a century or more. A seedling takes much longer and its nuts are a gamble, which is why a grafted tree is worth paying for.

faqs.q: How big does a pecan tree get, and is it too big for a suburban yard?
faqs.a: Pecans are large, long-lived shade trees, commonly 10 to 20 m tall and nearly as wide, with a deep taproot, so they are really a tree for a big garden, rural block or paddock rather than a small courtyard. There is no true dwarf pecan, so if space is tight, a macadamia is a more compact backyard nut tree.

faqs.q: Can I grow a pecan in a pot?
faqs.a: Not for long. A young pecan will sit in a large pot for a few years, but its deep taproot and eventual size mean it has to go into the ground to crop, so a pot is only a holding stage, never a permanent home.

faqs.q: Why is my pecan healthy but not setting nuts?
faqs.a: The usual reasons are that the tree is still too young, that it has no compatible partner to pollinate it, or that the summer is too cool or too short to fill the kernels. Heavy crops every second year (alternate bearing) and zinc shortage on alkaline soils also cut the harvest, so give it a pollinator, full sun, summer water and a spring zinc spray.

WA.intro: Growing pecans in Western Australia. WA plantings are small, but pecans have been trialled here for decades and suit the warm, dry inland and south-west where summers are long and hot and summer water is on hand.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pecans want a long, hot summer to fill their nuts rather than much winter chill, and the warm, dry parts of WA supply exactly that, with the bonus that dry summers all but rule out fungal disease. The Department of Agriculture's view is that pecans are well suited to WA conditions wherever the soil drains freely, though the industry here has only ever developed slowly. The cool, wet far south-west corner is the least suitable part of the state, both because the season is shorter there and because it would be the one zone at any scab risk if that disease ever reached Australia.

sections.heading: Where pecans grow in WA
sections.body: Older WA growing notes put the natural pecan range very wide, from north of Carnarvon and inland past Wiluna across to near Esperance, taking in most of the South-West apart from the wettest coastal strip. The best-documented WA work is the Department of Agriculture variety trial at the Stoneville research station in the Perth hills, begun in 1981 with cultivars imported from the eastern states, alongside earlier nut-crop trials further north. In practice WA pecans are scattered, backyard and small-orchard trees rather than a concentrated industry.

sections.heading: Varieties that suited WA trials
sections.body: The Stoneville trial rated Cape Fear, Cherokee, Wichita and Shoshoni among the cultivars with the most potential, and found Shoshoni and Cherokee came into bearing earlier than most. All of these are sold in Australia today. Because pecans are pollinated only by wind, the trial stressed that any variety needing cross-pollination has to be planted close to its partner, so for a WA backyard pair a Type A such as Cape Fear or Cherokee with a Type B such as Wichita or Shoshoni is a dependable combination.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA pecans flower in spring and ripen through the hot summer, so the nuts are gathered in autumn, generally from around April. As the husks split, knock or shake the nuts down, then crack and freeze the kernels.

sections.heading: Pests, and the scab-free advantage
sections.body: WA growers have it easy on disease: the dry summers and the fact that Australia is free of pecan scab mean pecans here need essentially no fungicide. Insect pests are few. The real competition for a backyard crop is cockatoos and parrots, which will strip a tree, so net it or harvest promptly. Mediterranean fruit fly is established across much of WA, but a hard-shelled nut is not a meaningful fruit fly host, so pecans are not a fruit fly concern.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the WA Organism List and are usually certified and treated before they can enter, and pecans face extra scrutiny as a way of keeping scab out. Many eastern nurseries will not post live trees to WA at all, so the in-stock table above is filtered to the sellers that can ship here.

faqs.q: Will a pecan get enough summer heat near the coast in Perth?
faqs.a: Cooler, breezier coastal spots can struggle to ripen and fill the nuts fully, because pecans live on summer heat. An inland or hills position that bakes through summer, with water kept up to the tree, gives much better-filled nuts than a mild coastal garden.

faqs.q: Are pecans suited to WA's sandy soils?
faqs.a: They tolerate sand but do not love it. A pecan drives a deep taproot and crops best in deep, fertile soil, so on Perth sand dig in plenty of organic matter, mulch well, water through summer and feed regularly, and accept that growth will be slower than on a good loam.

QLD.intro: Growing pecans in Queensland. Queensland is a secondary growing region by volume but the heart of the national industry's processing, and pecans do best here in the warm inland and southern districts under irrigation, not the humid tropical coast.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pecans need a long, hot growing season, around 200 to 220 frost-free days to mature a crop, and only a low winter chill (a few hundred hours below 7 degrees, which almost all of Queensland clears easily). The catch in Queensland is water and humidity rather than heat: the wet tropical coast is very humid and the trees want deep, free-draining soil and reliable irrigation, so the warmer inland and southern districts suit them far better than the coast. Australia's freedom from pecan scab removes the main disease worry that humidity would otherwise bring.

sections.heading: Where pecans grow in QLD
sections.body: Queensland pecan growing is centred on Mundubbera and Eidsvold in the central part of the state and on the southern districts from the Lockyer Valley down toward the New South Wales border, with smaller plantings noted around Bundaberg, Gatton, the Atherton Tablelands and Beaudesert. Queensland also hosts the country's largest pecan processing plant, at Toowoomba, which handles much of the national crop, so the state matters to the industry well beyond its own orchard area.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: As elsewhere, Queensland pecans flower in spring and ripen over summer, with the nuts harvested in autumn from about April into June; the warmest districts tend to open the season a little earlier.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: The headline is the disease that is not here: Australia, Queensland included, is free of pecan scab, so the crop is grown without the fungicide programs needed overseas. The insect pests that do matter in Australia, green vegetable bug, the maroga moth and longicorn borers, are managed by keeping trees healthy and by biological control. Queensland fruit fly is endemic, but the mature hard-shelled nut is not a meaningful host, so pecans are not a fruit fly concern. Birds, especially cockatoos, are the main backyard nuisance.

faqs.q: Can I grow pecans on the Queensland coast or in the wet tropics?
faqs.a: You can grow the tree, but the humid, high-rainfall coast is not ideal: pecans want sharp drainage and dislike constantly wet ground, and the coast is exactly where scab would bite hardest if it ever arrived. A warmer inland or southern site with deep, free-draining soil and irrigation gives more reliable, better-filled nuts.

faqs.q: Pecan or macadamia for a Queensland backyard?
faqs.a: A macadamia is the more compact, evergreen choice and is native to subtropical Queensland, so it suits a smaller, more humid coastal block. A pecan is a much larger deciduous tree that needs room and a long hot summer, so it is the better pick for a big inland or southern property.

NSW.intro: Growing pecans in New South Wales. NSW grows most of Australia's pecans, concentrated in the warm, irrigated valleys of the state's north, and it is effectively the home of the crop in this country.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pecans thrive where summers are hot and long, winters are cool but not severe, and irrigation water is reliable, which is precisely the climate of inland northern NSW. The humid north coast is wetter and would carry more disease risk if scab ever arrived, and the cold tablelands have too short a season to fill nuts well, so the inland river valleys are the sweet spot. Chill is never the limiting factor here; summer heat and water are.

sections.heading: Where pecans grow in NSW
sections.body: The great majority of the Australian crop is grown under irrigation in the Gwydir Valley east of Moree, where the Stahmann family's Trawalla orchard is the largest pecan operation in the southern hemisphere. Smaller plantings run from the Hunter Valley and Nelson Bay on the central coast up to the mid north coast near Kempsey and the north coast around Lismore. If you are planting in NSW you are growing the country's signature pecan crop in its home ground.

sections.heading: Water in NSW
sections.body: The inland crop is grown entirely under irrigation: the trees draw heavily on water through summer as the nuts develop and fill, and the deep alluvial soils of the river valleys hold and supply that water well. For a home grower the lesson is the same at any scale, water deeply and regularly through the hot months, because a pecan that runs short during nut fill drops its yield and quality.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Pecans flower in spring (around September), the nuts develop through the heat of summer, and harvest falls in autumn, typically May and June in the Gwydir district, where the nuts are mechanically shaken from the trees, swept up, then washed and sorted.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Pecan scab is an exotic, notifiable disease that is not present in Australia, so NSW pecans are grown without the heavy spraying it forces overseas. The insect pests of note here are green vegetable bug, the maroga moth and longicorn borers, kept in check by healthy trees and biological control. Queensland fruit fly is a host risk for soft fruit in the warmer parts of the state, but the hard pecan nut is not a meaningful host. Birds and cockatoos are the practical backyard pest.

faqs.q: Why are pecans grown around Moree rather than on the coast?
faqs.a: The inland north has the hot, long summers, cool winters and irrigation water pecans need, plus lower humidity, which keeps the trees healthy and the nuts well filled. The humid coast is wetter and would be more disease-prone, so the dry, irrigated inland valleys became the heart of the industry.

faqs.q: Can I grow a pecan in Sydney or the cool tablelands?
faqs.a: Sydney's warm-temperate climate will ripen pecans if you can give one tree the room and the summer water it needs. The cold tablelands are harder, because the growing season can be too short to fill the kernels, so expect lighter, later or poorly-filled crops there and choose the warmest, most sheltered spot you have.

VIC.intro: Growing pecans in Victoria. Victoria is marginal pecan country: the trees crop best in the warm, irrigated north and are unreliable where the summer is short or frosts are a risk.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The limit in Victoria is summer heat, not winter cold. Pecans need a long, hot season to ripen and fill their nuts, and much of Victoria is too cool or too short-seasoned for that, so the warm irrigated north is the realistic zone and the cool, frost-prone south is a gamble. Late spring frosts can damage the new growth and flowers and early autumn frosts can cut the season short before the nuts finish filling, so frost timing, rather than total chill, is what holds the crop back here.

sections.heading: Where pecans grow in VIC
sections.body: Victorian pecan plantings are small and concentrated in the warm irrigation districts of the north, in the Sunraysia and Murray Valley country around Mildura, among the other temperate nut crops grown there. Historically Victoria and New South Wales were the two established pecan states, but Victoria has always been a minor producer, and the cooler central and southern districts grow pecans only as occasional garden trees.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victorian pecans ripen later than the warmer northern states because the cooler season slows them down, so the autumn harvest tends to fall around May and June, and in a cool year the nuts can finish poorly filled if the season runs out before they mature.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Disease pressure is low: Australia is free of pecan scab, and Victoria's cooler, drier-summer inland gives fungi little chance anyway. Insect pests are few. The practical problems for a Victorian pecan are frost on young trees and the new season's growth, and birds, particularly cockatoos, taking the nuts, so shelter young trees and net the crop as it ripens.

faqs.q: Will a pecan ripen its nuts in Melbourne?
faqs.a: It is a stretch. Melbourne's summers are often too mild and the season too short to fill pecan kernels well, so even a healthy tree may set few nuts or poorly-filled ones. A hot, sheltered, north-facing spot helps, but the warm irrigated north of the state is far more reliable for a real crop.

faqs.q: Are pecans frost hardy in Victoria?
faqs.a: A dormant, established pecan handles winter cold well, but the vulnerable points are a late spring frost that burns the new growth and flowers, and an early autumn frost that stops the nuts filling. Protect young trees through their first winters and plant them where cold air drains away rather than pools.
